attributes.description: See the complete permission flow from login to database query to UI rendering.

post.html:
Full Flow Demo

Let's trace a complete permission-aware request through our system.

The Request

User editor@acme.com loads the document list.

Step 1: Authentication

// Middleware extracts user from session
req.user = {
  id: 2,
  email: "editor@acme.com",
  role: "editor",
  orgId: 1, // Acme Corp
}

Step 2: Build Permissions

// In route handler
const permissions = createDocumentPermissions(req.user)

// This runs the builder:
allow("read", "Document", { orgId: 1 }) // Their org
allow("read", "Document", [
  "id",
  "title",
  "content",
  "status",
  "createdAt",
  "authorId",
])
allow("create", "Document")
allow("update", "Document", { authorId: 2, orgId: 1 }) // Their docs

Step 3: Generate Database Filter

const filter = permissions.filter("read", "Document", documentsTable)
// Generates: WHERE orgId = 1

Step 4: Query with Filter

const documents = await db.select().from(documentsTable).where(filter)
// Returns only Acme Corp documents

Step 5: Filter Response Fields

const response = documents.map((doc) => permissions.filterFields(doc))
// Each doc has: id, title, content, status, createdAt, authorId
// Removed: orgId, internalNotes (editor can't see these)

Step 6: Client Receives Data

[
  { "id": 1, "title": "Q4 Report", "authorId": 1, "status": "published" },
  { "id": 2, "title": "Draft Ideas", "authorId": 2, "status": "draft" }
]

Step 7: React Renders with Permissions

// Client builds same permissions from user context
{
  documents.map((doc) => (
    <div key={doc.id}>
      <h3>{doc.title}</h3>

      <Can action="update" subject={doc}>
        {/* Only shows for doc.id=2 (their doc) */}
        <button>Edit</button>
      </Can>
    </div>
  ))
}

Result

  • User only sees Acme documents (org filter)
  • Sensitive fields are hidden (field filter)
  • Edit button only appears on their own documents (UI check)
  • Backend would reject edit on doc #1 anyway (defense in depth)

This is defense in depth: multiple layers all enforcing the same rules.
post.markdown:
# Full Flow Demo Let's trace a complete permission-aware request through our system. ## The Request User `editor@acme.com` loads the document list. ## Step 1: Authentication ```typescript // Middleware extracts user from session req.user = { id: 2, email: "editor@acme.com", role: "editor", orgId: 1, // Acme Corp } ``` ## Step 2: Build Permissions ```typescript // In route handler const permissions = createDocumentPermissions(req.user) // This runs the builder: allow("read", "Document", { orgId: 1 }) // Their org allow("read", "Document", [ "id", "title", "content", "status", "createdAt", "authorId", ]) allow("create", "Document") allow("update", "Document", { authorId: 2, orgId: 1 }) // Their docs ``` ## Step 3: Generate Database Filter ```typescript const filter = permissions.filter("read", "Document", documentsTable) // Generates: WHERE orgId = 1 ``` ## Step 4: Query with Filter ```typescript const documents = await db.select().from(documentsTable).where(filter) // Returns only Acme Corp documents ``` ## Step 5: Filter Response Fields ```typescript const response = documents.map((doc) => permissions.filterFields(doc)) // Each doc has: id, title, content, status, createdAt, authorId // Removed: orgId, internalNotes (editor can't see these) ``` ## Step 6: Client Receives Data ```json [ { "id": 1, "title": "Q4 Report", "authorId": 1, "status": "published" }, { "id": 2, "title": "Draft Ideas", "authorId": 2, "status": "draft" } ] ``` ## Step 7: React Renders with Permissions ```tsx // Client builds same permissions from user context { documents.map((doc) => (

{doc.title}

{/* Only shows for doc.id=2 (their doc) */}
)) } ``` ## Result - User only sees Acme documents (org filter) - Sensitive fields are hidden (field filter) - Edit button only appears on their own documents (UI check) - Backend would reject edit on doc #1 anyway (defense in depth) This is defense in depth: multiple layers all enforcing the same rules.
